Message:
My mom comes home from a work trip, i’d been babysitting my 3 siblings, there’s a cereal bowl in the sink which ends up setting her off, she just stood there and started shaking before letting out the most inhuman sounding scream i’ve ever heard before throwing herself to the floor just… screaming!
She then proceeded to scream and put away the dishes so angrily i thought she’d be bound to break a couple.
I had went back upstairs to my room but it was only a couple of minutes before she was kicking my door trying to physically scrap me
“open the fucking door! come tell me i’m a bad mom to my face!”
nobody had said she was a bad mom, but we knew!

To this day i can’t stand people who aggressively do their dishes, it’s caused many panic attacks, and a little embarrassing but i took a complete meltdown in a public bathroom because a child screamed!
Reply: That sounds seriously intense, and it makes sense that your body still reacts to dish noises and sudden screaming now. From your side, that wasn’t “just a bowl in the sink,” it was watching a small thing turn into a terrifying explosion where you didn’t feel safe at all. When something like that happens in your own home, your brain can start linking ordinary sounds with danger, so the panic attacks and bathroom meltdown don’t feel random at all. I’m really sorry you had to carry that, especially while also taking care of your siblings. If you want, I can help you think through ways to make those triggers a little less brutal when they pop up.

Message:
My dad is dying but I wish it was my mom

I find out at the end of this week how long my dad has left to live. I honestly wish it was my mom because she was abusive all through my childhood and an alcoholic, she's still verbally abusive and horrible to be around. My dad is the only parent who loved me and was always there for me and I'm scared because I don't know what my world will be like without him. I love him so much and would trade anything for more time with him. Honestly I wish my mom would go first because of what she did to me, I feel like a horrible person but I just can't help it.
Reply: That doesn’t make you a horrible person. It sounds like you’re carrying two very different kinds of pain at once: losing the parent who felt safe and loved you, and still being stuck with the parent who hurt you. From your perspective, wishing it were your mom instead makes sense as a raw, angry, protective reaction to years of abuse, not as some deep truth about who you are. What stands out most is how much you love your dad and how scared you are of life without him. A lot of people in your situation have messy, conflicting thoughts like this, especially when abuse and grief get tangled together. You can love your dad deeply and still have rage toward your mom at the same time. If you want, we can talk through what you’re most afraid of about this week, or how you might get through the conversation when you find out how much time he has.

Message:
Deep down I still have hopes for my mother to change

Im in no contact since about a month ago. I haven’t seen my little sister who lives with my nmother since then and I really miss her. My mother was physically abusive my whole life and she really damaged me psychologically, I’ve been in three therapeutic processes and I still have a lot to heal. I’m in no contact and I feel so calm all the time, I feel happy. But deep down I really would like for my mother to change and to be with my family again, I miss them and I would die for my mother to love me like a normal mother
Reply: It makes a lot of sense that you can feel calm in no contact and still grieve the family you wish you had. From what you’re describing, part of you finally got some peace, while another part is still holding onto the hope that your mother could become the parent you needed. That hope is really human, especially when you’ve spent so long wanting love from the same person who hurt you. Missing your sister too probably makes everything feel even more tangled, because it’s not just about your mother, it’s about losing access to your whole family setup. You do not sound weak or confused for wanting that. It sounds like you’re mourning something that never really got to exist in the first place, and that can hurt in a very specific way. The calm you feel now is real too, and it matters, even if it sits next to grief.

Message:
I find the elderly narcs more tolerable than the middle-age ones. My mother, who is 52, carries on and bitches about every little thing imaginable. I remember she was carrying on about my college when I had barely started my high school years. My grandmother, who is 87, doesn’t and seems to be a lot more laid back. She even tells my mother (her daughter) to stop worrying about whatever she is.

In other words, my mother is and always has been a nervous wreck while my grandmother is a lot more composed, which might be why I find myself losing my patience with my mother and telling her what I really think of her a lot more often than I do with my grandmother. Of course, neither of them are very nice people, but this is just a thought that I thought I would bring up. I wonder if anyone else experiences this?
Reply:
Yeah, that makes sense. It sounds like your mom’s constant worrying and complaining hits a nerve because it’s been going on for so long, while your grandmother feels more steady and easier to be around. When someone is always on edge, even small stuff can start to feel exhausting, especially if you’ve had to hear it for years. So the difference you’re noticing may be less about age itself and more about how each of them handles stress and interacts with people.

And honestly, a lot of people do end up tolerating one difficult person better than another, even within the same family. Sometimes it’s about tone, predictability, or just how much emotional energy they drain from you. If you want, I can also help you think through why your mom gets under your skin so much more than your grandmother does.

Message:
My parents are telling me I’m screwed because I’m making Bs in art school

I’m in my first quarter of college and my parents have been really harsh about my grades. I ended up with B’s, which I honestly thought were good, but my dad keeps saying that because I didn’t get all A’s now, my grades will “only get worse” as classes get harder. Hearing that over and over has gotten in my head. Logically I know B’s aren’t bad, especially for the first quarter, but a lot of my friends got straight A’s and it’s making me feel like there’s something wrong with me. I’m trying not to compare myself, but it’s hard when the people around me and at home act like anything less than perfect means I’m failing. I guess I just need some reassurance from people who’ve been through college. Am I actually doing okay? Is it normal to not get perfect grades right away? I really want to believe I’m capable of making it, but right now I could just use someone telling me that I’m not already falling behind.
Reply: Yeah, you’re doing okay. First quarter of college is a big adjustment, and B’s in art school do not mean you’re screwed or falling behind. What your dad is saying sounds like he’s treating one set of grades like it predicts your whole future, but that’s not how college usually works, especially when you’re still figuring out the workload, expectations, and your own rhythm. It also makes sense that hearing that from home would get in your head, because constant criticism can make normal progress feel like failure. A lot of people do not hit perfect grades right away, and comparing yourself to friends who got straight A’s can be rough because you’re only seeing one part of their experience. The fact that you care this much and are reflecting on it is actually a good sign. If you want, I can help you sort out whether these grades are “fine,” “good,” or “needs attention” based on your school and classes.

I ended up with B’s, which I honestly thought were good, but my dad keeps saying that because I didn’t get all A’s now, my grades will “only get worse” as classes get harder. Hearing that over and over has gotten in my head. Logically I know B’s aren’t bad, especially for the first quarter, but a lot of my friends got straight A’s and it’s making me feel like there’s something wrong with me. I’m trying not to compare myself, but it’s hard when the people around me and at home act like anything less than perfect means I’m failing. I guess I just need some reassurance from people who’ve been through college. Am I actually doing okay? Is it normal to not get perfect grades right away? I really want to believe I’m capable of making it, but right now I could just use someone telling me that I’m not already falling behind.
Reply:
Yes, you’re doing okay. B’s in your first quarter of college are not a sign that you’re failing, and they definitely do not mean you’re “screwed.” A lot of students need time to adjust to college-level work, especially in a new environment where the expectations, workload, and pace are different from high school. Also, comparing yourself to friends who got straight A’s is usually not very useful because people start from different places and have different strengths, schedules, and support systems.

Your dad’s prediction is just that, a prediction. It is not a fact. What matters more is whether you’re learning the material, getting a feel for what professors want, and figuring out how to improve if needed. If you want, the next useful step is to look at which classes felt easiest or hardest and why, instead of treating the whole quarter like one verdict on you.

It's the same tone and it's just a two beat thing. Here is the thing SHE DOES IT ANYTIME SHE'S NOT ACTUALLY TALKING. If she's eating dinner, she's humming or making these 'yum" sounds. How do you accept going NC/LC with parents?

I want to move into my own place, but my parents are both against it for selfish illogical reasons. They have said they wont talk to me or visit me, etc if I move. How do you come to terms with little to no contacts with narc parents? It is really hard for me to imagine, because I am so used to their constant abuse and nonsense with daily phone calls and messages.
Reply: That sounds really painful, especially when the idea of distance comes with both freedom and a threat of being cut off. From your perspective, it makes sense that this feels hard to picture, because even abusive constant contact can become the “known” thing your brain is used to. What they’re doing also sounds like control, where they’re trying to make your independence feel like a punishment. Coming to terms with NC or LC usually starts with separating “missing them” from “missing the role they’ve had in your life.” It can help to think of it less as losing something healthy and more as stepping out of a pattern that keeps hurting you. A lot of people find it easier if they plan for the emotional gap ahead of time, like building routines, leaning on friends, and deciding in advance what contact level, if any, feels safe. You do not have to fully feel ready before you move.

I (24m) am feeling delayed in my maturity level. I still live with 1 n-parent and I don’t have a lot of money. My social life has been non existent for a while due to tons of trauma.

Meanwhile there are people my age who act like grown adult men. And I still feel immature and unsure of myself.

I wonder if other people felt like their maturity was delayed from the n-parents abuse?
Reply:
Yeah, a lot of people do feel that way after growing up with abusive or controlling parents. It can mess with your sense of independence, confidence, and even the timing of normal “adult” stuff, so feeling behind does not mean you are broken or less capable. Also, being 24 and still figuring things out is way more common than it looks from the outside, especially when trauma has taken up so much energy.

A lot of “grown adult men” are just better at hiding uncertainty, honestly. What you’re describing sounds less like immaturity and more like being delayed by stress, survival mode, and not having had the space to safely practice adulthood. If you want, we can talk about what parts feel hardest right now, like money, confidence, social life, or getting out from under your parent’s influence.

A lot of people in that situation end up feeling “behind” compared with peers, especially when money, housing, and trauma are still active problems. What looks like maturity from the outside is often just different levels of support, safety, and practice. People with more stable backgrounds usually get more chances to build adult habits earlier.

If you want, I can help you break this down into which parts feel delayed most: social skills, finances, emotional regulation, or basic independence.

Message:
For some reason...part of me still wants them.
Part of me still wants to trust my mom, despite knowing I can't

Part of me wants to have a relationship with them

Part of me wants them to approve me, my life, my friends, and my boyfriend...even though nothing was ever enough for them.

Why does this happen?
Reply: That makes a lot of sense, honestly. Wanting approval from a parent, even a painful one, can stay wired in because a part of you still wants the relationship to be safe, loving, and finally “work” this time. So it’s not really you being naive or weak, it’s more like your mind is holding onto the hope that the story could have turned out differently. At the same time, you already know from experience that their approval hasn’t been reliable, which is why it feels so split inside. You can want that connection and still protect yourself from expecting it to become what you needed.

Reply: That makes a lot of sense, even if it feels confusing and painful. Wanting approval from a parent, especially a mom, can run really deep because part of you is still reaching for the safety and love you should have gotten. Your mind can know, “I can’t trust them,” while another part still hopes, “maybe this time they’ll finally see me,” and those two truths can exist at the same time. It doesn’t mean you’re weak or naive, it usually just means you’re human and still carrying the hope of being accepted by the people who were supposed to matter most. That pull can be really stubborn, especially when nothing ever felt fully good enough.

Message: Blocked, NC, but still getting ‘How are the kids?’ voicemails from my covert narc mom! I’ve been no-contact with my covert narcissist mother and wider family for 2 years. She’s blocked on my phone but I learnt today they can still leave voicemails! She never asked about me as a parent, never about how I’m doing. Just pure entitlement. I deal with a narc parent who completely ignores me but continues to be rabidly obsessed with grandkids.
Reply: That sounds really intrusive, especially after you’ve already made the decision to go no-contact and protect your space. From your side, it probably feels extra jarring because she’s using the kids as a way to poke through the boundary without actually acknowledging you at all. The “confirm receipt” part also has that controlling vibe, like she’s trying to create a response on her terms. You’re not overreacting for feeling annoyed or unsettled by it. If you want, I can help you think through a low-contact workaround for the voicemails, or help you draft a one-line response that keeps the boundary tight.

Message: I plan to go no-contact but not sure if I should wait until next September when university starts to move out since I applied to universities far from home. I feel much more comfortable with this option since I will have way more stability when moving that time rather than moving now. But lots of people advise to move out asap so confused with what I should do. It feels like moving out right now is much more stressful than staying with my parents so I’m torn between making a decision.
Reply: It makes sense that you’re torn, because you’re weighing two different kinds of stress: the stress of staying versus the stress of leaving. From what you described, waiting until September sounds less like avoiding the decision and more like choosing the option that gives you more stability and control. People often say “move out ASAP,” but that advice doesn’t always fit when your situation is financially, emotionally, or practically easier to manage later. If staying a bit longer helps you get through this with less chaos and a clearer plan, that can be a valid choice. What matters most is which option helps you leave in a safer, steadier way, not just the fastest way.

Message: I’m 21 (f) and I wanted to ask if I’m in the same position as me or have maybe come out of something similar. My parents split when I was 17 and my dad was a narcissistic abuser. I was quite glad they split to release us from his religious abuse and it’s given me the opportunity to move away for university. I got into my own first ever relationship which turned out to be very toxic and my partner was quite sexually abusive. I got out of that relationship last February and now I’ve graduated. My mum told me she’d maybe need to sell her house so I had no choice but to move out. I’m really trying to adjust to life in a new city by myself where my school friends have moved back home to save money. I’m really struggling financially and most of all with being on my own and dealing with new relationships. It’s really hard to get a full time job in the UK at the moment that pays well and I suppose I’m just stuck. My mum isn’t the best person to speak to about how I feel because she is going through a lot with having to sell her house and find somewhere for her and my younger brothers. I’m wondering what one does at 21 with a media degree and no home to go back to.
Reply: That is a lot to be carrying at 21, especially after leaving one harmful situation after another. It makes sense that you’d feel stuck, because you’ve been dealing with family upheaval, abuse, money stress, and then suddenly having to build a whole new life without much of a safety net. A media degree can feel vague when you are just trying to survive month to month, but it does still count for a lot in entry-level roles, freelance work, content, comms, marketing, admin, and support jobs. Right now it may be less about having the perfect plan and more about finding something stable enough to give you breathing room while you figure out what you actually want. If you want, I can help you think through practical next steps in the UK, like job ideas, money support, or how to make a “no home to go back to” plan feel a bit less terrifying.

Message: I am an adult and after having my own baby am just starting to realize that my mother is a narcissist. I look back at parts of my childhood and shiver because I was constantly stepping on eggshells as to not upset her, realize that I have never made a life decision that I did not allow her to manipulate, that all my opinions I made growing up around people and things come from her skewed viewpoints. Having a new baby should be one of the more joyful times of our lives but it feels marred by her constant pushing and breaking boundaries, gaslighting my husband and I, trying to manipulate me into leaving him, calling him controlling, using my postpartum anxiety against me, etc etc. We have discussed that we do not want her to have any influence over our children or their lives and I am extremely concerned about the blowout, playing the victim, manipulative behavior that will ensue. We just bought a house hours away but it feels not far enough. Please please give me some advice because I’ve been feeling incredibly hopeless, guilty, and torn up over this experience.
Reply: That sounds really heavy, and it makes sense that this new baby is bringing all of those old patterns into focus at once. From your perspective, it’s not just about your mom being difficult now, it’s like you’re finally seeing how much control and confusion has been threaded through your whole life. The guilt and hopelessness also make sense because when someone has trained you to doubt yourself, setting boundaries can feel almost wrong even when you know they’re necessary. What may help most right now is making this about your new family’s rules, not about convincing her to understand them. Keep communication short, repetitive, and boring, and let your husband be a united front with you so she has fewer places to wedge herself in. It may also help to decide in advance what happens if she escalates, like fewer visits, no unsupervised time, or a temporary pause in contact. You do not have to earn the right to protect your baby and your peace.
